name: Sequana
description: Sequana was a Gallo-Roman goddess primarily associated with the source of the Seine River (Sequana). Her cult flourished in the region of modern-day Burgundy, France, during the Roman period. Archaeological evidence, particularly from the sanctuary at Source-Seine, provides insights into her veneration. Sequana was believed to possess healing powers, and pilgrims journeyed to her sacred spring seeking cures for various ailments. Votive offerings, including wooden and metal effigies representing afflicted body parts, were left at the sanctuary as appeals for divine intervention. The iconography of Sequana often depicts her in a boat, symbolizing her dominion over the river and her role as a guide or protector. The spring itself was considered a liminal space, a gateway between the earthly realm and the divine, where communication with Sequana was possible. Her worship represents a syncretic blend of indigenous Celtic beliefs and Roman religious practices, demonstrating the adaptability and resilience of local traditions under Roman rule. She is a powerful symbol of the river's life-giving properties and the enduring human connection to the natural world.
subtitle: Goddess of the Seine

keyMyths.title: The Discovery of Sequana's Spring
keyMyths.description: Legend tells of a shepherd who, stricken with a debilitating illness, wandered aimlessly until he stumbled upon a hidden spring. He drank from its waters and bathed in its flow, and miraculously, his sickness vanished. He realized that the spring was sacred, blessed by the goddess Sequana, and dedicated his life to tending to the site, guiding others to her healing waters. This myth highlights Sequana's association with healing and the origin of her sanctuary.

keyMyths.title: Sequana's Journey Down the Seine
keyMyths.description: It is said that Sequana would travel the length of the Seine in her sacred boat, bestowing blessings upon the land and its people. She would ensure the river's flow remained strong, providing sustenance and prosperity. During times of drought or hardship, the people would pray to Sequana, offering gifts in hopes of restoring the river's bounty and receiving her divine favor. This myth emphasizes Sequana's role as a provider and protector of the Seine region.
keyMyths.source: Green, Miranda. *The Gods of the Celts*.

keyMyths.title: The Votive Offerings to Sequana
keyMyths.description: Many pilgrims, afflicted with various ailments, would journey to Sequana's sanctuary at the source of the Seine. They would bring with them votive offerings, often crafted from wood or metal, depicting the afflicted body part. These offerings were left at the spring as a plea to Sequana for healing and relief from suffering. The act of offering symbolized a surrender to the goddess's power and a hope for her divine intervention. The archaeological record at Source-Seine provides ample evidence of this practice.
keyMyths.source: Deyts, Simone. *Images des Dieux de la Gaule*.

sources.author: Green, Miranda
